revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:日本原住民論]][[ko:일본원주민론]][[zh:日本原住民論]]
{{Unreferenced|date=April 2010}} {{Otheruses|The historical theories advocated by the historical novelist {{ill|Yagiri Tomio|ja|八切止夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yagiri Tomio|1=Tomeo Yagiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11390586}} since the 1960s|The archaeological theory of the Meiji period known as {{ill|Pre-Ainu theory|ja|プレ・アイヌ説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pre-Ainu theory|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135579531|1=Q135579531}} and its lineage|{{ill|lt=Korobokkuru theory|Korpokkur#Anthropologic_and_folkloric_discussions|ja|コロポックル#コロポックル論争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Korobokkuru theory|1=Korpokkur|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2471355}}}}
'''Theory of Japan's Indigenous People''' (日本原住民論)
# A series of theories collectively referred to as "Yagiri's historical perspective," with a focus on the "Japanese indigenous people" as defined by historical novelist {{ill|Yagiri Tomio|ja|八切止夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yagiri Tomio|1=Tomeo Yagiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11390586}}. # The historical perspective of Japan's New Left, which borrows and modifies Yagiri's perspective. Both are discussed in this article.
----
== Yagiri's Historical Perspective == Yagiri Tomio proposed various theories that overturned conventional historical wisdom. Among them was the "Theory of Japan's Indigenous People," which posited that the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|大和朝廷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Court|ja_comment=|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|1=Yamato Kingship|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2658252}} was an external regime and that the descendants of indigenous peoples became the {{ill|Burakumin|ja|部落民|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Burakumin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 部落問題|1=Burakumin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q548787}} and {{ill|Sanka (ethnic group)|en|Sanka (ethnic group)|qid=Q4833074|lt=Sanka|1=Sanka (ethnic group)}} populations.<ref>The idea that the Yamato government was an external regime and that the origins of Burakumin trace back to ancient ethnic groups such as the Emishi predates Yagiri, who combined these theories.</ref>
However, Yagiri's perspective assumed that these "indigenous peoples" were historically connected to the Imperial lineage, with the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|lt=Imperial House of Japan|1=Imperial House of Japan}} serving as a symbol of their unity. Thus, Yagiri himself was not a proponent of leftist ideology.<ref>In Yagiri's book *History of Japan's Indigenous People*, respectful expressions towards the Imperial family, such as "His Majesty Emperor Sujin (97 BCE, pg. 13)" and "Prince Hieda, Crown Prince of Emperor Kōnin (pg. 17)," demonstrate a clear distinction from New Left ideology.</ref><ref>However, Yagiri's theories, including the idea that the Emperor descended from Arabs, show inconsistencies.</ref>
== New Left Historical Perspective == The "Theory of Japan's Indigenous People" was later adopted by Japan's New Left, unbeknownst to Yagiri. During his time underground, Communist League Red Army faction leader {{ill|Umenai Tsuneo|ja|梅内恒夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umenai Tsuneo|1=Tsuneo Umenai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11538394}} was inspired by Yagiri's theory and published *To All Who Aspire to Overthrow Japanese Imperialism*. This marked a shift in the justification for "overthrowing Japanese imperialism" from the wrongdoings of the {{ill|Empire of Japan|en|Empire of Japan|qid=Q188712|lt=Empire of Japan|1=Empire of Japan}} since the Meiji era to the persecution of indigenous peoples at the time of Japan's founding. {{ill|Ryu Ota|en|Ryu Ota|qid=Q7385483|lt=Ryū Ōta|1=Ryu Ota}}, who advocated the theory of revolutionary poverty, also praised Yagiri as a "true people's historian."
Thus, New Left activists established a unique historical perspective that called for the "restoration of Japan's indigenous people." Their approach involved elevating the "phantom regional regimes" destroyed by the Yamato Court to relativize the Emperor system. This historical perspective inspired {{ill|Saburō Katō (New Left)|ja|加藤三郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saburō Katō (New Left)|1=katou saburou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11398971}} and other {{ill|Non-sect radical|ja|ノンセクト・ラジカル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Non-sect radical|qq=}} activists to commit anti-Japanese terrorist acts and piqued their interest in the {{ill|Koshikoden|ja|古史古伝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koshikoden|1=koshi koden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11410976}}, texts claiming to predate the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|qid=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}.
Some New Left activists,<ref>Besides Ryū Ōta, former {{ill|Bund|en|Bund|qid=Q128590|lt=Bund movement activists|1=Bund}} like {{ill|Takeda Sōgen|ja|武田崇元|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Sōgen|1=Sūgen Takeda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11546163}} are notable examples.</ref> influenced by pseudo-histories, contributed to the occult boom of the 1980s. The popularity of texts like the {{ill|Tōno outside the Three Counties|ja|東日流外三郡誌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōno outside the Three Counties|1=Tsugarusotosangunshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11527216}} was no coincidence. According to researcher {{ill|Hajime Harada (author)|ja|原田実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hajime Harada (author)|1=harada minoru|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q16284037}}, movements like {{ill|Aum Shinrikyo|en|Aum Shinrikyo|qid=Q217408|lt=Aum Shinrikyō|1=Aum Shinrikyo}}, which also emerged during this time, were pseudo-historical movements influenced by these ideologies, leading to attempts at {{ill|coup d'état|ja|国家転覆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/coup d'état|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 転覆|1=tenpuku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11637535}}.
== Historical Interpretations == Although the New Left historical perspective borrows heavily from Yagiri's interpretations, their unique aspect lies in using these interpretations as a political ideology to rationalize anti-Japanese thought. Consequently, historical facts that do not align with their perspective are often ignored or altered.
; Origins of the Ainu people :Yagiri saw the {{ill|Ainu people|ja|アイヌ民族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainu people|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アイヌ|1=Ainu people|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q101828}} as a people native to Hokkaido from ancient times, separate from the "Japanese indigenous people."<ref>Later, Yagiri proposed a theory that white Ainu subjugated yellow-skinned indigenous people but were collectively conquered by newcomers from the continent.</ref> The New Left, however, included the Ainu as part of the "Japanese indigenous people," using their "enslavement" as a basis for condemning "Japan's wrongdoing." ; Origins of the Imperial House :Both perspectives consider the Imperial House to have originated from {{ill|Toraijin|en|Toraijin|qid=Q11561926|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin}} rather than the "Japanese indigenous people." The New Left uses this to deny the Emperor's legitimacy as a ruler. ; Origins of the Fujiwara clan and the ritsuryō system :The New Left claims that the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|en|Battle of Baekgang|qid=Q608727|lt=Battle of Baekgang|1=Battle of Baekgang}} led to a Tang occupation, which established the ritsuryō system and rewrote history through the compilation of the Nihon Shoki, thus asserting their dominance.
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} <references/>
== References == * Yagiri Tomio, *History of Japan's Indigenous People*, Asahi Shimbun Publishing, 1972. * Ryū Ōta, *Japan's Indigenous People and the Emperor System*, Shinsensha, 1982. * Hajime Harada, *The Fantasy of the Arahaba Secret History*, Hihyōsha, 1999.
== See also == * {{ill|Yagiri Tomio|ja|八切止夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yagiri Tomio|1=Tomeo Yagiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11390586}} * {{ill|Ainu_Revolution_Theory|en|Ainu_Revolution_Theory|qid=Q11282359|lt=Ainu Revolution Theory|1=Ainu Revolution Theory}} * {{ill|Anti-Japaneseism|en|Anti-Japaneseism|qid=Q4774213|lt=Anti-Japaneseism|1=Anti-Japaneseism}} * {{ill|Koshikoden|ja|古史古伝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koshikoden|1=koshi koden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11410976}} * {{ill|Pseudohistory|en|Pseudohistory|qid=Q11146759|lt=Pseudohistory|1=Pseudohistory}} * {{ill|Buraku origins debate|ja|部落の起源論争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buraku origins debate|1=Buraku origins debate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11643264}} * {{ill|Nashinoki Shrine#Bombing|ja|梨木神社爆破事件|lt=Nashinoki Shrine Bombing|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nashinoki Shrine#Bombing|qq=}} * {{ill|Iron King|en|Iron King|qid=Q6072636|lt=Iron King|1=Iron King}} * {{ill|Korobokkuru theory|ja|コロボックル説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korobokkuru theory|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コロポックル|1=Korpokkur|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2471355}}
== External links == * [http://www.asyura.com/sora/bd/msg/327.html "The Other 'Charismatic' Leader I Met" Part 1 by Hajime Harada] * [http://www.asyura.com/sora/bd/msg/328.html "The Other 'Charismatic' Leader I Met" Part 2 by Hajime Harada]
{{translated page|ja|日本原住民論|version=101540188|comment=Imported full ja history}}
